primary_diagnosis.name: Non-bullous impetigo of the face, extensive and rapidly spreading, aggravated by topical corticosteroid misuse

differential.evidence_basis: Honey/golden-crusted superficial lesions on an erythematous base, rapidly spreading across the face after minor nasal trauma during a URTI — the classic picture.

A rapidly spreading facial vesicular-crusted rash is the exact presentation of a dermatological emergency; classically monomorphic punched-out vesicles/erosions, often with systemic upset — cannot be fully excluded on a phone camera.
Perioral location fits, but grouped vesicles on a single erythematous base with serous (not honey) crusting and confinement to the vermilion border would be expected.
Itch and facial erythema fit, but frank golden purulent crusting and rapid contiguous spread point to infection rather than eczema.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At opening, the model should hold impetigo as most likely while explicitly keeping eczema herpeticum/HSV as the must-not-miss that changes the disposition.

differential.evidence_basis: Honey-crusts, painless mildly-itchy superficial lesions, no systemic toxicity, clear oral mucosa, portal of entry after nasal trauma — coherent for impetigo.

Made less likely by the absence of systemic upset, absence of underlying atopic dermatitis flare, painlessness and honey (not serous) crusting — but not excludable without a clinician's eyes on the lesion morphology.
Purulent honey-crusting plus regional lymphadenopathy indicate a primary infectious process, not an allergic one.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After history the leading diagnosis is impetigo; the safe disposition still hinges on prompt in-person visualisation to exclude eczema herpeticum and to prescribe.

differential.evidence_basis: Clinical/visual diagnosis; swab for MC&S confirms organism and guides therapy but empiric treatment should not wait. Presumptive via telehealth, confirmed on in-person review.
Retained as the must-not-miss requiring in-person exclusion; if grouped monomorphic punched-out vesicles or systemic upset were present, this becomes an emergency needing antiviral therapy.

differential_progression.scoring_note: A correct final position names impetigo as the working diagnosis AND preserves eczema herpeticum as the reason a prompt in-person look is required rather than blind chat management.

red_flags.description: Rapidly spreading facial vesicular-crusted rash that cannot be reliably distinguished from eczema herpeticum / disseminated herpes simplex on a phone camera
red_flags.clinical_significance: Eczema herpeticum is a dermatological emergency needing systemic antivirals; a spreading facial rash must be visualised in person to exclude it before committing to purely antibacterial home management.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Untreated eczema herpeticum can disseminate, involve the eye (herpetic keratitis) and cause serious systemic illness.

red_flags.description: Facial/perinasal location with potential for spread toward the eye and periorbital cellulitis
red_flags.clinical_significance: Perinasal and midface infection can track to the periorbital tissues; eye swelling, pain or visual change signals periorbital/orbital cellulitis requiring emergency care.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Progression to orbital cellulitis threatens vision and can spread intracranially.

red_flags.description: Iatrogenic aggravation — topical corticosteroid applied to an untreated bacterial skin infection
red_flags.clinical_significance: Topical steroid locally suppresses immunity and accelerates bacterial spread; continuing it worsens the infection. The correct plan must stop it.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Ongoing steroid use allows continued rapid spread and a larger, slower-healing infection.

red_flags.description: Signs of deep or systemic infection (spreading hot tender erythema, fever with rigors) indicating cellulitis or bacteraemia
red_flags.clinical_significance: Currently absent (afebrile, painless) — but their development converts a superficial infection into an urgent/emergent one.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Untreated cellulitis or systemic sepsis from Staphylococcus aureus or Streptococcus pyogenes.

pathophysiology_summary: Non-bullous impetigo is a superficial epidermal infection, usually by Staphylococcus aureus and/or Streptococcus pyogenes, seeded through a breach in the skin barrier — here, minor trauma to the nares from repeated nose-blowing during a viral URTI. Bacterial colonisation of the anterior nares is a common reservoir. Superficial vesicopustules rupture and dry into the characteristic thick, adherent honey/golden crusts on an erythematous base. Spread is by autoinoculation (scratching) and contact, hence the rapid contiguous extension and high contagiousness. Applied topical corticosteroid impairs local innate immunity and accelerates spread. Regional lymphadenopathy reflects the local immune response. Streptococcal strains carry a small risk of post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis (not rheumatic fever) weeks later. Extensive or multi-lesion facial disease is treated with a systemic anti-staphylococcal/streptococcal antibiotic plus crust removal and strict infection control; localised disease can be treated topically.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Anchoring on the classic honey-crust picture and failing to keep eczema herpeticum / disseminated HSV as a must-not-miss that needs in-person exclusion, especially with a facial location.
Missing the iatrogenic trap — not eliciting or not acting on the over-the-counter hydrocortisone use, which must be stopped, not continued or escalated.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Under-treating extensive disease with topical-only therapy
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Recommending topical antibiotic alone when the lesions are extensive/multiple across the face, where a systemic antibiotic is indicated.
Committing to management purely over chat without arranging a prompt in-person look at a rapidly spreading facial rash — the safe path is a prompt prescriber/in-person assessment.
Treating a well, afebrile adult with classic impetigo as a 000/ED emergency — over-triage; the correct level is prompt same-day assessment, not an ambulance.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Definitive lesion morphology to distinguish honey-crusted impetigo from monomorphic punched-out herpetic vesicles (needs a clinician's close inspection)
Presence and tenderness of regional (submandibular/anterior cervical) lymphadenopathy — requires palpation
Precise blood pressure, heart rate and oxygen saturation without a home device
Bacterial swab microscopy/culture and sensitivities (Staphylococcus aureus vs Streptococcus pyogenes)
Whether early deep/soft-tissue (cellulitis) involvement is developing beneath the crusts
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Arrange a prompt same-day in-person / prescriber assessment to visualise the lesions, exclude eczema herpeticum/HSV and periorbital involvement, and prescribe a systemic antibiotic; give infection-control advice and clear safety-netting in the interim. Not an emergency ambulance in a well, afebrile patient.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Moderate — the honey-crust picture is characteristic, but the real telehealth risk is committing to purely antibacterial home care over chat while missing eczema herpeticum, periorbital spread, or the need to stop the steroid.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Localised non-bullous impetigo: topical antibiotic (e.g. mupirocin). Widespread or multiple lesions, or systemic features: oral anti-staphylococcal/streptococcal antibiotic (di/flucloxacillin, or cephalexin if penicillin-hypersensitive). Remove crusts, strict hygiene, exclude until 24 hours of effective antibiotic therapy.
